title: RELIEF Act
hook: This bill aims to help K-12 students in low-income areas get after-school tutoring and food.

sections.title: TL;DR

items.label: What
items.text: Creates grants for after-school tutoring and nutrition for K-12 students.

items.label: Who
items.text: Sponsor, party, and state not specified in the bill text.

sections.expanded_detail: This bill would establish a new grant program through the Department of Education. These grants would help non-profit organizations offer after-school tutoring and meals to K-12 students in low-income communities. While specific sponsor details are not available in the bill text, a bill at this 'Introduced' stage has just been filed and must undergo review by committees before any votes can occur.

sections.title: Where Is This Bill?
sections.expanded_detail: This bill is currently in the 'Introduced' stage, meaning it has been formally submitted to the House of Representatives. Before it can become law, it typically needs to be assigned to a committee for review, debate, and potential amendments. If approved by the committee, it would then be voted on by the full House, followed by a similar process in the Senate, and finally signed by the President.

sections.title: If This Passes, You Might Notice

bullets: More after-school programs could open in low-income areas.
K-12 students might receive tutoring and nutritious meals after school.
Non-profit organizations could get federal funding for eligible programs.

sections.expanded_detail: If this bill becomes law, non-profit organizations meeting specific criteria (like operating for 3+ years and serving high-poverty areas) could apply for federal funding. This funding would allow them to expand or create after-school programs offering academic support and nutritious food for K-12 children. This could lead to improved educational outcomes and better access to food for students in underserved communities, starting in fiscal year 2026.

sections.title: The Debate

collapsed_summary.supporters: Supporters would likely highlight the bill's potential to address critical educational disparities and food insecurity among K-12 students.
collapsed_summary.critics: Critics might raise concerns about the program's cost, administrative overhead, or the role of federal funding in local education initiatives.

sections.expanded_detail: This bill aims to provide resources to communities struggling with childcare and education costs. Those in favor would point to the direct support it offers to students and families in need. Those against might debate the best way to achieve these goals or the overall level of federal spending.

sections.title: Bottom Line
sections.collapsed_summary: This bill creates a grant program to fund after-school tutoring and meals for K-12 students in high-poverty areas.
sections.expanded_detail: The bill authorizes $50 million annually for five years, starting in 2026, to help non-profits offer vital support to children beyond the school day.
